examples.title: FCFS Disk Scheduling Algorithm
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS_Schedule(requests, start_track)
BEGIN
    total_seek <- 0
    current_track <- start_track
    
    FOR each request IN requests DO
        seek_distance <- ABS(request - current_track)
        total_seek <- total_seek + seek_distance
        current_track <- request
        PRINT "Serviced request at track: " + request
    END FOR
    
    PRINT "Total Seek Distance: " + total_seek
    PRINT "Average Seek Time: " + (total_seek / length(requests))
END
examples.explanation: FCFS processes disk requests in the order they arrive in the queue. It starts from the current head position and calculates the absolute distance to each request, summing up all seek distances to get total head movement.

examples.title: SCAN (Elevator) Disk Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of SCAN disk scheduling algorithm
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SCAN_Schedule(requests, start_track, max_track, direction)
BEGIN
    sorted_requests <- SORT(requests)  // Sort in ascending order
    total_seek <- 0
    current_track <- start_track
    
    // Find position to start scanning
    IF direction = "forward" THEN
        right_requests <- sorted_requests WHERE > start_track
        left_requests <- sorted_requests WHERE <= start_track
        
        // Service all forward requests
        FOR each req IN right_requests DO
            total_seek <- total_seek + ABS(req - current_track)
            current_track <- req
        END FOR
        
        // Go to end and reverse
        total_seek <- total_seek + ABS(max_track - current_track)
        current_track <- max_track
        
        // Service remaining (backward) requests
        FOR each req IN reverse(left_requests) DO
            total_seek <- total_seek + ABS(req - current_track)
            current_track <- req
        END FOR
    END IF
    
    PRINT "Total Seek Distance: " + total_seek
END
examples.explanation: SCAN algorithm works like an elevator - it services requests in one direction until it reaches the end, then reverses direction and services remaining requests. This reduces average seek time compared to FCFS while providing fair service.

examples.title: SSD Wear Leveling Simulation
examples.description: Python simulation demonstrating basic wear leveling concept
examples.code:
import random

class SSDSimulator:
    def __init__(self, num_blocks, p_e_cycles):
        self.num_blocks = num_blocks
        self.p_e_cycles = p_e_cycles
        self.block_wear = [0] * num_blocks
        self.logical_to_physical = {i: i for i in range(num_blocks)}
        self.physical_to_logical = {i: i for i in range(num_blocks)}
    
    def write(self, logical_block, data):
        """Write data with wear leveling"""
        # Find least worn block
        min_wear = min(self.block_wear)
        available_blocks = [i for i, w in enumerate(self.block_wear) if w == min_wear]
        
        # Choose a random block from least worn
        new_physical = random.choice(available_blocks)
        
        # Update mappings
        old_physical = self.logical_to_physical[logical_block]
        self.logical_to_physical[logical_block] = new_physical
        self.physical_to_logical[new_physical] = logical_block
        
        # Increment wear count
        self.block_wear[new_physical] += 1
        self.block_wear[old_physical]  # Old block available for reuse
        
        print(f"Written to logical block {logical_block} -> physical block {new_physical}")
        print(f"Block wear counts: {self.block_wear}")
    
    def get_wear_stats(self):
        return {
            'min': min(self.block_wear),
            'max': max(self.block_wear),
            'avg': sum(self.block_wear) / len(self.block_wear)
        }

# Demo usage
ssd = SSDSimulator(num_blocks=8, p_e_cycles=1000)
for i in range(20):
    logical_block = random.randint(0, 7)
    ssd.write(logical_block, "data")
    
print(f"\nWear Statistics: {ssd.get_wear_stats()}")
examples.explanation: This code simulates wear leveling in SSDs. When writing data, it always selects the least-worn block to write to, ensuring even distribution of writes across all blocks to maximize SSD lifespan.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of blocks

examples.title: Disk Access Time Calculator
examples.description: C program to calculate total disk access time components
examples.explanation: This C program calculates the total disk access time by combining seek time (head movement), rotational latency (waiting for the sector to rotate under the head), and transfer time (actual data transfer). It demonstrates the mathematical formulas used in disk performance analysis.
